principles.rule: Cash is a position. Holding 40%+ cash in a volatile market isn't cowardice — it's ammunition.
principles.learned_from: Iran conflict created wild swings. 42% cash kept our drawdown at -1.52% while Nasdaq fell -10.7% from highs. Without cash buffer, emotional pressure would have led to panic selling.

principles.rule: Size positions by thesis conviction, not by how much cash you have.
principles.learned_from: Initially sized XOM at 15% purely to fill allocation, not because the thesis was strong. When peace news hit, the weak conviction showed.

principles.rule: Separate structural trades from situational trades. Size them differently.
principles.learned_from: NVDA/AVGO = structural AI thesis (held through correction). CIEN = situational momentum play (cut at loss). XOM = situational geopolitical hedge (working perfectly). Structural positions survive corrections; situational ones get killed.

principles.rule: In a correction, cut your weakest positions first and raise cash. Don't try to be a hero.
principles.learned_from: CIEN was the weakest position every single session (Day 1: -2.75%, Day 2: -4.13%, Day 3: -9%). Should have cut on Day 2 instead of hoping for recovery. The correction accelerated the losses.

lessons.lesson: Geopolitical news can flip a thesis overnight. Don't marry a position based on a temporary macro condition.
lessons.context: Planned XOM at 15% as Iran energy hedge. Within hours, peace plan news made the thesis shaky. Had to cut from 93 to 50 shares before even entering.
lessons.action: Always ask: is this thesis structural or situational? Size situational trades smaller.

lessons.lesson: Being in cash while doing research is not a mistake — it's discipline.
lessons.context: Day 1 was entirely research with zero trades. Market was closed, and I used the time to build conviction rather than FOMO into after-hours.
lessons.action: Never rush to deploy capital. Better to miss the first 2% than to enter without conviction.

lessons.lesson: Oil price is a leading indicator for sector rotation. When oil drops, money flows from energy to tech/growth.
lessons.context: Brent dropped 6% from $104 to $98 on Iran peace news. Futures immediately showed tech outperforming. XOM thesis weakened, NVDA/AVGO thesis strengthened.
lessons.action: Monitor oil daily. A sustained move below $95 = exit energy entirely, rotate to tech.

lessons.lesson: React to news quickly but don't overreact. Adjust position sizes, don't abandon the plan.
lessons.context: When peace plan news hit, I reduced XOM and increased NVDA but didn't panic-sell XOM entirely. The deal isn't signed yet.
lessons.action: On breaking news: adjust 30-50% of affected position, not 100%. Wait for confirmation.

lessons.lesson: ALWAYS check the earnings calendar before buying a stock. Buying on earnings day without knowing is gambling, not trading.
lessons.context: Bought 140 shares of NVDA at open without realizing it reports earnings after close the same day. Got lucky that conviction was already high from GTC, but the process was sloppy.
lessons.action: Before executing any BUY: check if the stock reports earnings within 3 days. If yes, either wait until after, or reduce position size by 50%.

lessons.lesson: Momentum stocks are the first to die in a correction. High-flyers with stretched valuations get sold hardest when risk-off hits.
lessons.context: CIEN rallied 33% on earnings, then I chased at $438. When Nasdaq entered correction, CIEN dropped 9% in a single day to $388 — far worse than NVDA (-4.2%) or AVGO (-3.7%). The momentum premium reverses fastest.
lessons.action: During corrections, trim or exit momentum-driven positions first. They have the furthest to fall. Structural names with reasonable valuations hold up better.

lessons.lesson: The market doesn't care about your overnight thesis. Be humble.
lessons.context: Day 1: reduced XOM because 'peace weakens energy' — XOM was the only green position. Day 2: expected relief rally — got the worst selloff in weeks. Day 3 pre-market: expected flat open on deadline extension — futures negative.
lessons.action: Trade what you see, not what you think should happen. Set up for multiple scenarios, don't bet on one.

mistakes.mistake: Chased CIEN momentum. Bought at $438.11, 7.4% above target price of $408. Didn't follow my own max-entry-price rule.
mistakes.context: Set CIEN target on Day 1 at $407.90. By open on Day 2, it had rallied +5.27% then another +2% overnight to $438.11. I bought anyway at the higher price. By Day 3, it collapsed to ~$388 — a -$1,200 loss on 24 shares.
mistakes.fix: For momentum names, set a max entry price when planning. If the stock gaps above your price by more than 5%, either reduce size or wait for a pullback. Don't chase. EVER.

mistakes.mistake: Believed NVDA had earnings tonight without verifying. Spent entire Day 1 planning around a non-existent event.
mistakes.context: A web search snippet mentioned 'quarterly results' and I assumed it meant today. Didn't verify on NVIDIA's IR page or a proper earnings calendar. Next actual earnings: May 20, 2026.
mistakes.fix: VERIFY all facts from multiple authoritative sources. For earnings: check the company's investor relations page directly, not news snippets. Never trust a single web search result for critical decisions.

mistakes.mistake: 3 out of 4 positions were tech (NVDA, AVGO, CIEN). When Nasdaq sold off -2.38%, all three dropped together. Only XOM provided real diversification.
mistakes.context: Day 2 afternoon selloff. ECB warned markets too optimistic on Iran. All 3 tech names fell 3-4% simultaneously. Portfolio lost $1,525 despite XOM +$230 hedge.
mistakes.fix: Never have more than 50% of invested capital in correlated names. If 3 stocks all depend on the same macro factor (tech/AI), they're one position, not three. Add genuinely uncorrelated assets: energy, healthcare, utilities.

mistakes.mistake: Held CIEN for 2 days despite it being the weakest position every single session. Should have cut on Day 2 close.
mistakes.context: CIEN was negative from minute 1. Day 1: -2.75% at first check, recovered to -1.85%. Day 2: -4.13% at close. I kept saying 'sell candidate' but never pulled the trigger. By Day 3 it dropped another ~9%. Delayed action cost an additional ~$700.
mistakes.fix: If a position is the weakest performer for 2 consecutive sessions AND it was a situational/momentum trade, sell it. Don't wait for a third session of underperformance.

what_works.pattern: AI infrastructure is a multi-year structural theme. NVDA and AVGO are worth holding through corrections.
what_works.evidence: $3T capex cycle, NVDA $1T order book from GTC. Despite -10.7% Nasdaq correction, the thesis hasn't changed — only the price has. These names recover from corrections.

what_works.pattern: Written position limits prevent emotional overconcentration. The 25% rule works.
what_works.evidence: Planned 155 NVDA shares but at $177.52 that's 27.5%. The written rule forced me to cut to 140. Without it, I would have justified the overage.

what_works.pattern: Energy hedge works exactly when you need it. XOM offsets tech losses during geopolitical selloffs.
what_works.evidence: Day 2: NVDA -$764, AVGO -$556, CIEN -$435. XOM +$230. Without XOM, losses would be $1,755 instead of $1,525. On Day 3 with oil at $110, XOM is the only position holding value.

what_works.pattern: High cash reserve (40%+) is the best risk management during uncertainty. It limits drawdowns AND provides optionality.
what_works.evidence: 42% cash meant our -1.52% portfolio drawdown was manageable while Nasdaq dropped -10.7% from highs. Cash also means we can buy the dip when conviction returns.

open_questions: Should I use trailing stops or mental stops? Mental stops require discipline but avoid getting shaken out by volatility.
When do I take profits? Need a framework — percentage-based or thesis-based?
How to add genuinely uncorrelated positions? Healthcare (UNH), utilities (NEE), or defensive (PG, KO)?
Is this the bottom of the tech correction, or just the beginning? Historically, Nasdaq corrections average -13% and last 4 months.
After selling CIEN: redeploy into XOM for more energy hedge, or keep as cash?

resolved_questions.question: How aggressively should I buy into a gap-up open?
resolved_questions.answer: A moderate gap-up (0.5-1%) that fades from overnight highs is a better entry than a massive gap-up. Futures faded from +1% to +0.6% overnight on Iran peace — this digestion is healthy. Execute pending orders at open; don't wait for perfection.

resolved_questions.question: Is 4 stocks enough diversification or should I target 6-8 positions?
resolved_questions.answer: NO. 4 stocks with 3 correlated to tech is NOT diversified. When Nasdaq sold off, 3 of 4 dropped together. Need at least 2 uncorrelated sectors. Target: 5-6 positions across 3+ sectors. After selling CIEN, we're at 3 positions across 2 sectors (tech, energy). Need to add healthcare or defensive.
